diagrams.title: BFS vs DFS Traversal Comparison
diagrams.description: A directed graph with 6 nodes (A-F) and directed edges showing the difference between Breadth-First Search and Depth-First Search traversal orders. BFS visits level by level (A â†’ B, C â†’ D, E, F) while DFS explores deeply before backtracking (A â†’ B â†’ D â†’ E â†’ C â†’ F). This visualĺŻąćŻ”helps students understand how the two traversal strategies differ in node exploration order.
elements: 6 circular nodes labeled A, B, C, D, E, F positioned to show a branching structure
Directed edges: Aâ†’B, Aâ†’C, Bâ†’D, Bâ†’E, Câ†’F
BFS traversal order annotation: A, B, C, D, E, F
DFS traversal order annotation: A, B, D, E, C, F
Color legend showing BFS (blue) vs DFS (red) paths
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrates the fundamental difference between BFS (queue-based, level-order) and DFS (stack-based, deep exploration) traversal strategies on the same graph structure.

diagrams.title: Topological Sort - Course Prerequisites DAG
diagrams.description: A Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) representing a typical course prerequisite structure: Data Structures (DS) â†’ Algorithms (Algo) â†’ Advanced Algorithms (AdvAlgo), and Parallel prerequisites. Shows valid topological orderings like: Math â†’ DS â†’ Algo â†’ AdvAlgo, or Math â†’ DS â†’ Parallel â†’ Algo â†’ AdvAlgo. Nodes are courses with directed edges showing prerequisite relationships.
elements: 6 course nodes: Math, Data Structures, Algorithms, Advanced Algorithms, Parallel Computing, Machine Learning
Directed edges representing prerequisites: Mathâ†’DS, DSâ†’Algo, DSâ†’Parallel, Algoâ†’AdvAlgo, Algoâ†’ML, Parallelâ†’ML
Topological order displayed below: Math â†’ Data Structures â†’ Algorithms â†’ Advanced Algorithms
Dependency arrows clearly showing 'must be taken before' relationship
Cyclic warning indicator showing NO cycles (acyclic property)
diagrams.purpose: Illustrates how topological sort produces a valid ordering for directed acyclic graphs where all edges point from earlier to later items, commonly used for task scheduling and dependency resolution.

diagrams.title: Kahn's Algorithm - In-Degree Based Topological Sort
diagrams.description: Step-by-step visualization of Kahn's algorithm using the course prerequisite graph. Shows in-degree counts for each node, the queue of zero in-degree nodes, and the removal process. Step 1: Queue = [Math] (in-degree 0), Step 2: Remove Math, Queue = [DS] (in-degree becomes 0), Step 3: Remove DS, Queue = [Parallel, Algo], continuing until all nodes are processed.
elements: In-degree counter displayed inside each node (e.g., 'Math: 0', 'DS: 1', 'Algo: 2')
Queue visualization box showing nodes with in-degree 0
Arrow showing 'remove node' action and in-degree updates
Final sorted order output box: Math â†’ DS â†’ Parallel â†’ Algo â†’ ML â†’ AdvAlgo
Step labels: Step 1, Step 2, Step 3, Step 4, Step 5, Step 6
diagrams.purpose: Explains Kahn's algorithm conceptually: repeatedly removing nodes with zero in-degree (no dependencies) to produce a topological ordering, reinforcing the connection between in-degree and topological sorting.
